scenario: Iris Retailer runs a Camunda Spring Boot Starter worker on Camunda 8.8 that processes 2900 orders per hour. The developer needs to read BPMN service-task custom headers such as routingKey from the handler without enlarging the fetched variable list, so they pick the documented parameter annotation that exposes the headers as a Map and leaves variable fetching behaviour unchanged.
question: What does the @CustomHeaders annotation provide on a @JobWorker handler parameter?

options.text: It binds a Map<String, Object> of process variables active on the calling token, and it adds those variable names to the joint list of variables that the worker fetches
It binds a Map<String, String> of custom headers defined on the activated job, and the docs note it does not have any effect on the variable fetching behaviour for the worker
It binds a JobClient instance positioned at the activated job, and the docs note that using it as a parameter prevents the implicit variable fetching detection on the handler method
It binds an ActivatedJob holder for the current job, and the docs note that using it as a parameter prevents the implicit variable fetching detection on the handler method

a.text: Incorrect. @CustomHeaders binds custom headers as a Map<String, String>, not process variables, and the docs explicitly state it does not affect variable fetching.

b.text: Correct. The docs state @CustomHeaders is used on a Map<String, String> parameter to retrieve custom headers for a job and that this will not have any effect on the variable fetching behaviour of the worker.

c.text: Incorrect. JobClient is a separate parameter type from the native JobHandler interface; @CustomHeaders is the annotation for a Map of custom headers.

d.text: Incorrect. ActivatedJob is a separate parameter type and is the one that prevents implicit variable fetching detection; @CustomHeaders has no such effect.

explanation: The @CustomHeaders annotation binds a Map<String, String> parameter to the custom headers of the activated job. The docs state that using @CustomHeaders has no effect on the variable fetching behaviour of the worker.
